title: Non-Hermitian Computers Need No Complex Numbers
abstract: In traditional quantum computing, it has been established that real quantum computation augmented with non-Clifford gates is as powerful as universal quantum computation. Here we investigate this phenomenon in the non-Hermitian setting. We show that a non-Hermitian quantum computer equipped with the real gate set ${H, \text{CCNOT}, G}$, where $G = \operatorname{diag}(g^{-1}, g)$ with $g > 0$ and $g \neq 1$, can solve problems in $\text{P}^{\sharp\text{P}}$ in polynomial time, matching the capability of its universal non-Hermitian counterpart ${H, T, \text{CNOT}, G}$. This demonstrates that non-unitarity, rather than universality, is the essential resource, and that complex numbers are unnecessary.
summary: This paper proves that complex numbers are not necessary to achieve the high computational power associated with non-Hermitian quantum computing. By showing that a real gate set can solve PP-complete problems, the author demonstrates that the essential resource for this class of quantum advantage is non-unitarity itself.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether complex numbers are an essential resource for the enhanced computational power provided by non-Hermitian quantum computing.
main_result: The authors prove that a real gate set {H, CCNOT, G} is as powerful as the universal non-Hermitian gate set, establishing that BRNQP = BNQP = P^#P and demonstrating that non-unitarity, rather than complex numbers, is the key resource.
method: The study uses complexity theory and algorithmic construction, specifically designing a circuit to solve the PP-complete MAJSAT problem and using path-sum expansion to relate success probabilities to the P^#P complexity class.
model_or_system: A non-Hermitian quantum computer (NQC) model that relaxes the unitarity restriction, utilizing a gate set including the non-unitary gate G = diag(g^-1, g).
key_observables: The outcome of the measurement of the BHR control qubit in the x direction (+1 vs -1).
important_parameters: The amplification strength parameter g (where g > 1), the ratio beta/alpha encoded in the BHR qubit, and the number of satisfying assignments s.
main_assumptions: The construction assumes a precision-controlled constant to manage the spacing of real numbers and assumes the ability to perform non-unitary operations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the implementation of an X gate using CCNOT and ancillae; Figure 2 illustrates the decomposition of multi-control Toffoli gates; Figure 3 shows the circuit realization of a 3-CNF clause.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the context of real vs complex gates in Hermitian computing, then introduces the non-Hermitian model, provides a constructive algorithm for solving MAJSAT using real gates, and concludes with a formal complexity-theoretic proof of the equality of the complexity classes.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it identifies non-unitarity as the fundamental driver of computational advantage, independent of the presence of complex phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
